1: 'The scatterer has come up against you. Man the ramparts; watch the road; dress for battle; collect all your strength.' The opening of ch. 2 is a mock military instruction to Nineveh — ironically directing the city to prepare its defenses against the coming assault. The scatterer (mepit, one who scatters/disperses) is the Babylonian-Median coalition that would besiege and destroy Nineveh in 612 BCE. The rapid-fire military imperatives (man, watch, dress, collect) create a breathless urgency that highlights the futility of Nineveh's preparation.
2: 'For YHWH is restoring the majesty of Jacob as the majesty of Israel, for plunderers have plundered them and ruined their vines.' The theological grounding of the military narrative: YHWH restores the ge'on (pride/majesty) of Jacob, which Assyrian plundering had stripped away. The vine imagery (ruined vines) connects to the agricultural devastation of Assyrian campaigns through Israel and Judah. The restoration of Jacob's dignity is the reason Nineveh falls — not Babylonian imperial ambition but divine justice.
3: 'The shield of his mighty men is red; his soldiers are clothed in scarlet. The chariots come with flashing metal on the day he musters them; the cypress spears are brandished.' The battlefield scene shifts to the attacking army's appearance. Red shields (perhaps leather dyed red or blood-soaked) and scarlet uniforms — possibly referring to Median warriors who wore distinctive garments. The flashing metal of the chariots catches the light. The cypress spears (beroshim) are the long-shafted weapons made from fir/cypress wood. The battle description is visual and kinetic.
4: 'The chariots race madly through the streets; they rush to and fro through the squares; their appearance is like torches; they dart like lightning.' The chaotic chariot charge through Nineveh's streets during the assault. Like torches (kelappidim) and like lightning (kaberaqim) — the light imagery intensifies the visual chaos. Ancient Nineveh had wide processional streets; the image of enemy chariots racing through what had been royal parade routes is deliberately humiliating.
5: 'He remembers his officers; they stumble as they go; they hurry to the wall; the siege tower is set up.' Nineveh's defenders scramble — the officers trip over themselves in haste. The siege tower (sukkat) erected against the wall indicates the attackers are pressing the siege. This verse is ambiguous about whose side is described (defenders or attackers); the stumbling and scrambling suggest the panic of Nineveh's defenders trying to mount a counter-response.
6: 'The river gates are opened; the palace melts away.' This verse is remarkable for its potential historical specificity. Ancient accounts (Diodorus Siculus, drawing on earlier sources) report that during Nineveh's siege, the Khoser River flooded and breached part of the city's defenses, allowing the attackers entry. Whether or not Nahum had specific foreknowledge, the image of river gates opening connects the fall to YHWH's flood judgment announced in 1:8. The palace melting (vomuag) suggests either literal destruction or royal dissolution/collapse.
7: 'It is decreed that the city be exiled, carried away; its slave women lead her like the sound of doves, beating their breasts.' The exile of Nineveh's population — the mirror of the exile Assyria had imposed on Israel (2 Kgs 17:6; 18:11). The feminine personification of the city (common in prophetic literature) with her slave women mourning is the exact reversal of Assyrian deportation reliefs: the women who had watched foreign captives march past now march themselves. The sound of doves — the cooing mourning cry of doves was a standard comparison for lamentation (Isa 38:14; 59:11; Ezek 7:16).
8: 'Nineveh is like a pool whose waters run away. Halt! Halt! they cry, but no one turns back.' The pool of water draining is an image of the city's population fleeing — unstoppable, like water pouring out. The guards' cry to halt the fleeing citizens is futile. This image may also carry ironic force: Nineveh, which had received Jonah's preaching and repented, now faces the judgment that the earlier repentance had only delayed. The water imagery connects to the river gates of v. 6 and the flood of 1:8.
9: 'Plunder the silver, plunder the gold! There is no end of the treasure or of the wealth of all precious things.' The battle cry of the victorious attackers plundering Nineveh's legendary treasury. Nineveh was one of the ancient world's wealthiest cities — capital of an empire that had looted the ancient Near East for over a century. The accumulated tribute from Israel, Judah, Egypt, Babylon, and dozens of other kingdoms filled its storehouses. The rhetorical no end of treasure acknowledges Nineveh's wealth while announcing its transfer to the conquerors.
10: 'Desolation and ruin! Hearts melt and knees tremble; anguish is in all loins; all faces grow pale.' The aftermath of the assault: four terms of devastation — buqah umebuqah umevullaqah (a play on similar-sounding destruction roots) — emptied, plundered, desolated. The physical symptoms of total panic (melting hearts, trembling knees, anguish in loins, pale faces) are the reverse of the battle-ready posture commanded in v. 1. These same symptoms appear in the description of Babylon's fall in Isa 13:7-8.
11: 'Where is the lions' den, the feeding place of the young lions, where the lion and lioness went, where his cubs were, with none to disturb them?' The lion was Assyria's royal symbol — Assyrian palace art depicted the king as hunter and lion-companion; lion hunts were royal prerogatives. The multiple lion terms (aryeh, labi, kefir, gur) catalog the whole pride — king, queen, cubs — asking where this supposedly invincible family now is. The rhetorical 'where?' (ayeh) is the question of mourning and aftermath.
12: 'The lion tore enough for his cubs and strangled prey for his lionesses; he filled his caves with prey and his dens with torn flesh.' The recent past of Assyrian predatory empire: the lion (Assyria/the king) hunted for his whole family, filling the den with plunder. The den filled with prey is an image of Nineveh stocked with the plunder of nations. The past tense describes what was; the contrast with the empty-den question of v. 11 makes the point: the den is now empty, the pride gone.
13: 'Behold, I am against you, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will burn your chariots in smoke, and the sword shall devour your young lions. I will cut off your prey from the earth, and the voice of your messengers shall no longer be heard.' YHWH speaks directly: hinni elayich (I am against you) — the divine declaration of war against a city, appearing also in Ezek 26:3 (against Tyre) and Jer 50:31 (against Babylon). The military assets are burned; the next generation of warriors cut off; the empire's ambassadors silenced. The cessation of Nineveh's diplomatic voice — its tribute demands, its threatening messages — is the specific relief for Judah that 1:15 announced.
